my_sentence: A veterinarian are educated than landscaper.
correction: Veterinarians are better educated than landscapers.
explanation: A comparison needs a comparative form plus 'than': say 'better educated', not just 'educated'. Also, to talk about a profession in general, use the bare plural (veterinarians / landscapers) and make the verb agree (are). 'A veterinarian are' mixes a singular subject with a plural verb.
correct_examples: Engineers are better paid than teachers. | City doctors are better trained than rural ones.
context: Comparing how educated vets and landscapers are.

my_sentence: Become more a veterinarian.
correction: become a veterinarian
explanation: Drop the extra 'more' — it doesn't belong here. To say you enter a profession, the pattern is 'become a + job'. The teacher kept repeating just 'Become.' to signal that the extra words were unnecessary.
correct_examples: He wants to become a vet. | She became a teacher after college.
context: Explaining how to become a veterinarian.

my_sentence: anyone went to the college can choose this major
correction: anyone who went to college can choose this major
explanation: You need the relative pronoun 'who' to link the clause to 'anyone'; without it the sentence has two verbs and no connection between them. Also drop 'the': 'go to college' (like 'go to school') takes no article when it means attending as a student.
correct_examples: Anyone who finishes the course gets a certificate. | Students who go to college can apply for this scholarship.
context: Saying anyone can choose the landscaping major.

my_sentence: I need to focus my English skills and my family.
correction: I need to focus on my English skills and my family.
explanation: 'Focus' meaning 'concentrate on' always takes the preposition 'on': focus on something. Don't drop it.
correct_examples: Let's focus on the main problem. | This year I want to focus on my health.
context: Explaining why he has no time for movies.

my_sentence: Only my daughter or my wife want to watch a movie, and then I will company with them.
correction: I will accompany them.
explanation: 'Company' is a noun; the verb meaning 'go somewhere with someone' is 'accompany'. It is transitive, so it takes a direct object with no 'with': 'accompany them', not 'company with them'.
correct_examples: I'll accompany you to the hospital. | She accompanied her mother to the market.
context: Saying he goes to movies only to be with his family.

my_sentence: They can't earn money. They need to ask money for their parents.
correction: They need to ask their parents for money. / They need to ask them for it.
explanation: The pattern is 'ask somebody for something': the person comes right after 'ask', then 'for + the thing'. 'Ask money for their parents' reverses the roles and means something else. (The teacher's 'ask it from their parents' also works.)
correct_examples: He asked his boss for a raise. | Don't be afraid to ask me for help.
context: Star fans are students who get money from their parents.

my_sentence: He built his own company, so he recruit many students. They recruit some students to help.
correction: He recruited many students. / They recruited some students to help.
explanation: When you narrate past events, keep the verbs in the past tense: recruit -> recruited. You dropped the past '-ed' several times in a row. Watch verb endings ('-ed' for past, '-s' for he/she/it) — it is a recurring pattern for you. See also [[2026-05-28-008]].
correct_examples: He recruited several interns last summer. | They hired students for part-time work.
context: Describing how an aerobics instructor staffed his company.

my_sentence: If someone become the soccer coach, they can earn a lot.
correction: If someone becomes a good soccer coach, they can earn a lot.
explanation: (assistant note) After a third-person singular subject ('someone'), the present-tense verb needs '-s': becomes. Use 'a' (a soccer coach) since you are not pointing to one specific, already-known coach. Same verb-ending weakness as [[2026-05-28-007]].
correct_examples: If someone becomes a doctor, they can help many people. | When she becomes a manager, she will travel a lot.
context: Arguing that good soccer coaches earn a lot.

my_sentence: Ground water means the water, we can see it on the ground.
correction: Groundwater is the water that you see on the ground.
explanation: Don't split a definition into two comma-spliced clauses ('the water, we can see it...'). Join them with a relative pronoun: 'the water that you see on the ground'. The teacher pointed out this is exactly what conjunctions and relative pronouns are for — keep a definition to one clean sentence.
correct_examples: A greenhouse is a place where you grow plants. | An eyewitness is a person who sees an event happen.
context: Defining 'groundwater' during the compound-noun lesson.

my_sentence: Sorry teacher, what's the public speaker?
correction: Excuse me, could you explain what a public speaker is? / Sorry, what does "public speaker" mean?
explanation: (assistant note) 'Sorry teacher' is a word-for-word translation from Chinese. To politely interrupt or ask, start with 'Excuse me' or 'Sorry,' and follow with a full question. Also, when asking what a type of thing is, use 'a' (general) rather than 'the' (a specific, already-known one): 'what's a public speaker'.
correct_examples: Excuse me, what does that word mean? | Sorry, could you explain what a barista is?
context: Asking the teacher to define 'public speaker'.

my_sentence: I think that's because there should are interesting and comedy.
correction: because they are interesting and humorous
explanation: Use 'they are' (subject + be), not 'there should are', which is ungrammatical. And describe the shows with the adjective 'humorous', not the noun 'comedy'. So: 'because they are interesting and humorous.'
correct_examples: I watch them because they are funny and relaxing. | The show is interesting and humorous.
context: Explaining why he watches stand-up comedy.

my_sentence: They can spend money for the soccer team.
correction: They can spend money on the soccer team.
explanation: (assistant note) The collocation is 'spend money on something', not 'for'. Use 'spend time/money on + the thing or activity'.
correct_examples: Fans spend a lot of money on their idols. | I don't want to spend money on things I don't need.
context: Talking about how much soccer fans spend.

my_sentence: Reading compound nouns as separate words: takeaway -> 'take her away', popcorn -> 'pop a coin', courthouse -> 'got our house', textbook -> 'text the book', eyeball -> 'I ball'.
correction: takeaway / popcorn / courthouse / textbook / eyeball — one blended word, stressed on the first part.
explanation: A compound noun is a single word: blend it together and don't insert extra syllables or articles in the middle. Most noun+noun compounds are stressed on the first element (TAKEaway, POPcorn, COURThouse, TEXTbook, EYEball). This was the focus of today's lesson.
correct_examples: POPcorn ['pɒpkɔːn], not pop-a-corn | TEXTbook ['tekstbʊk], not text-the-book
context: Reading-aloud drill for compound nouns.

my_sentence: There are many greenhouse there.
correction: There are many greenhouses there.
explanation: After 'many', a countable noun must be plural: many greenhouses. The teacher modeled the plural ('so many greenhouses there'). Same missing '-s' pattern as your verb endings in [[2026-05-28-007]] and [[2026-05-28-008]].
correct_examples: There are many greenhouses in my hometown. | She visited many countries last year.
context: Saying his hometown has many greenhouses.

my_sentence: We need to collect many information from anywhere.
correction: We need to collect a lot of information from anywhere.
explanation: (assistant note) 'Information' is uncountable: it has no plural and doesn't take 'many'. Use 'a lot of information' / 'much information', and never 'informations'.
correct_examples: We collected a lot of information for the report. | There isn't much information about it online.
context: Comparing CIA work to his company's security department.

my_sentence: When we meet someone we need to handshake with them.
correction: When we meet someone we shake hands with them.
explanation: (assistant note) 'Handshake' is a noun (the gesture). The action is the verb phrase 'shake hands with someone'. So 'shake hands with them', not 'handshake with them'. Same noun-used-as-verb slip as [[2026-05-28-005]] (company/accompany).
correct_examples: It's polite to shake hands with your host. | They shook hands and sat down.
context: Explaining what a handshake is.

my_sentence: Veterinarians are educated than landscapers.
correction: Veterinarians are better educated than landscapers.
explanation: A comparison needs a comparative form. 'educated than' is incomplete; use 'better educated than' (comparative of the adverb 'well') or 'more educated than'.
correct_examples: She is better educated than her brother. | This phone is more advanced than mine.
context: Reading the warm-up sentences comparing jobs.

my_sentence: There are too many traffics in it, so we want to reduce it.
correction: There is too much traffic, so we want to reduce it.
explanation: 'Traffic' is an uncountable (mass) noun: it has no plural 'traffics' and takes 'much' + a singular verb, not 'many' + 'are'. Say 'too much traffic'.
correct_examples: There is too much traffic during rush hour. | How much traffic was there on the way?
context: Describing a meeting about reducing traffic on their service.

my_sentence: During my visited my hometown, there were too many people.
correction: During my visit to my hometown, there were too many people.
explanation: After the possessive 'my' (and the preposition 'during') you need the noun 'visit', not the verb 'visited'. Use the pattern 'during my visit to + place'.
correct_examples: During my visit to Beijing, I saw the Great Wall. | On my first visit to the city, I got lost.
context: Telling a story about heavy traffic when going to his hometown.

my_sentence: There are too many people in the highway.
correction: There are too many people on the highway.
explanation: Use 'on' with roads, streets and highways, not 'in': 'on the highway / on the road'.
correct_examples: There was an accident on the highway. | Don't play on the road.
context: Describing crowds on the highway during a holiday.

my_sentence: It was happened, a serious traffic event.
correction: There was a serious car accident.
explanation: 'Happen' is intransitive and never passive, so 'was happened' is wrong. To introduce an event, use 'There was + noun' or 'A serious accident happened.'
correct_examples: There was a serious car accident on the highway. | A big earthquake happened last night.
context: Explaining why the traffic jam occurred.

my_sentence: Many people prepared this problem, so they bring a portable toilet.
correction: Many people are prepared for this problem, so they bring a portable toilet.
explanation: 'Prepare' needs the preposition 'for' before the thing you are getting ready against: 'be prepared for something'. 'prepared this problem' is missing 'for'.
correct_examples: You should be prepared for emergencies. | We prepared for the exam all week.
context: Talking about people getting ready for traffic jams.

my_sentence: We couldn't find any parking lot, so we went away.
correction: We couldn't find any parking lot, so we drove away.
explanation: When you leave by car, 'drive away' is more precise and natural than the general 'go away'.
correct_examples: He got angry and drove away. | We drove off without saying goodbye.
context: Making a sentence with 'parking lot'.

my_sentence: The dining table that I bought, I put on the network, was pretty expensive.
correction: The dining table that I bought online was very expensive.
explanation: Keep the relative clause together: 'the dining table (that) I bought online'. Don't split the subject with an extra clause, and 'buy online' is the natural phrase, not 'put on the network'.
correct_examples: The shoes I bought online were too small. | The car that he sold last week was very old.
context: Making a sentence with 'dining table'.

my_sentence: If you don't have an apartment in this area, you can't send your children to the high school.
correction: If you don't have an apartment in this area, you can't send your children to high school.
explanation: Institutions used for their normal purpose take no article: 'go to / send to school, high school, university, hospital'. 'the high school' would mean one specific building.
correct_examples: My son goes to high school. | She wants to go to university next year.
context: Making a sentence with 'high school'.

my_sentence: We can sit around the coffee table to have a rest.
correction: We can sit around the coffee table to rest.
explanation: 'Rest' is also a verb, so 'to rest' is shorter and more natural than 'to have a rest'. Use the verb directly after 'to'.
correct_examples: Let's stop and rest for a while. | I need to rest after work.
context: Making a sentence with 'coffee table'.

my_sentence: Under the moonlight, it's too difficult find the way go home.
correction: Under the moonlight, it's too difficult to find our way back home.
explanation: 'too + adjective' is followed by 'to + verb': 'too difficult to find'. Also use the set phrase 'find our way back home' with the possessive 'our'.
correct_examples: It was too dark to see anything. | We finally found our way back home.
context: Making a sentence with 'moonlight'.

my_sentence: We need to build a building to prevent earthquake and storm.
correction: We need to build a building with a stronger structure to prevent an earthquake or a storm from destroying the infrastructure.
explanation: Singular countable nouns need an article: 'an earthquake', 'a storm'. Also use 'prevent something from doing something' to say what the damage would be.
correct_examples: A vaccine prevents people from getting sick. | We must protect houses from being damaged by a storm.
context: Making a sentence with 'earthquake'.

my_sentence: People like to eat sweet food because we find it a good way to help us improve well being.
correction: People like to eat sweet food because they find it a good way to improve our well-being.
explanation: Don't drop possessive pronouns: it is 'improve our well-being', not 'improve well-being'. Keep the subject consistent too — 'people ... they', not 'people ... we'.
correct_examples: Exercise improves our well-being. | People should take care of their own health.
context: Making a sentence with 'well-being'.

my_sentence: It's a sunny day today, so the weather was pretty good.
correction: It's a sunny day today, so the weather is pretty good.
explanation: Use the present tense for a situation that is true now. Since it is sunny today, say 'the weather is pretty good', not 'was'.
correct_examples: It's raining now, so the roads are wet. | Today is hot, so the weather is uncomfortable.
context: Answering what the weather is like today.

my_sentence: There are fewer and fewer people becoming a bus driver.
correction: There are fewer and fewer people who become bus drivers.
explanation: When the subject ('people') is plural, the job noun must be plural too: 'become bus drivers'. With 'fewer and fewer people', a relative clause 'who become...' reads more naturally.
correct_examples: Fewer young people want to become teachers. | Many of them became doctors.
context: Making a sentence with 'bus driver'.

my_sentence: We have many automatic cars to instead them.
correction: We can use automatic cars to replace them.
explanation: 'Instead' is an adverb, not a verb, so 'to instead them' is wrong. The verb you want is 'replace' (replace someone/something). The phrase form is 'instead of'.
correct_examples: Machines are replacing workers. | We used tea instead of coffee.
context: Making a sentence with 'bus driver'.

my_sentence: A newspaper reporter need to face many serious problems.
correction: Newspaper reporters need to face a lot of problems.
explanation: A singular subject 'a reporter' takes 'needs', not 'need'. For people in general the plural is more natural: 'reporters need'.
correct_examples: Teachers need a lot of patience. | A teacher needs a lot of patience.
context: Making a sentence with 'newspaper reporter'.

my_sentence: More and more people gave up their career.
correction: More and more people give up their careers.
explanation: For a general, ongoing trend use the present simple 'give up', not the past 'gave up'. Also each person has a career, so use the plural 'careers'.
correct_examples: More and more people work from home these days. | Many young people give up their jobs to travel.
context: Making a sentence with 'newspaper reporter'.

my_sentence: The man we met yesterday is a choir director.
correction: The man we met yesterday was a choir director.
explanation: Because the time marker is 'yesterday', the main verb must be past: 'was a choir director'. Keep tenses consistent with the time you mention.
correct_examples: The woman I met yesterday was very kind. | The film we watched last night was boring.
context: Making a sentence with 'choir director'.

my_sentence: The choir director gave us a great performance, so we need to thanks for it.
correction: The choir director gave us a great performance, so we should be grateful for it.
explanation: 'Thanks' is informal and is not used as a verb this way. In a more formal sentence use 'be grateful for' or 'thank + someone'. '(be) appreciate' is also wrong — 'appreciate' takes a direct object: 'we appreciate it'.
correct_examples: We are grateful for your help. | I really appreciate your support.
context: Making a sentence with 'choir director'.

my_sentence: One of my friends is the orchestra conductor. He always join our meeting and give us interesting music.
correction: One of my friends is an orchestra conductor. He always joins our meetings and gives us interesting performances.
explanation: Third-person singular 'he' needs '-s': 'joins', 'gives'. Use 'an orchestra conductor' (one of several, not 'the'), and a conductor gives a 'performance', not 'music'.
correct_examples: He always helps his classmates. | She gives us a lot of homework.
context: Making a sentence with 'orchestra conductor'.
